Families of Valujet Crash Victims Find Lawyers Ignore Solicitation Ban
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Where there is a disaster like the crash of Valujet Flight 592, there will always be lawyers looking for clients. And if the Valujet crash is a guide, they cannot be stopped by the state bar's ban on solicitation of disaster victims and their families, nor by threats of disbarment. 

     In Florida, the ban, which the United States Supreme Court upheld last summer, extends to 30 days after a disaster. But within days of the Valujet crash in the Everglades on May 11, dozens of personal-injury lawyers had sent color brochures promoting their air-disaster expertise, addressed to the "family of" the crash victims, care of the Crown Sterling Suites in Miami, the hotel where the families stayed while officials first tried to recover the remains.

wiki_events.headline: The first flight of Ariane 5 explodes after roughly 37 seconds. It was a Cluster mission.

pages.title: Ariane 5
pages.abstract: Ariane 5 is a retired European heavy-lift space launch vehicle operated by Arianespace for the European Space Agency (ESA). It was launched from the Guiana Space Centre (CSG) in French Guiana. It was used to deliver payloads into geostationary transfer orbit (GTO), low Earth orbit (LEO) or further into space. The launch vehicle had a streak of 82 consecutive successful launches between 9 April 2003 and 12 December 2017. In development since 2014, Ariane 6, a direct successor system was first launched in 2024.

pages.title: Ariane flight V88
pages.abstract: Ariane flight V88, also known as Ariane 5 Flight 501, was the failed maiden flight of the Arianespace Ariane 5 rocket, vehicle no. 501, on 4 June 1996. It carried the Cluster spacecraft, a constellation of four European Space Agency research satellites.

pages.title: Cluster II (spacecraft)
pages.abstract: Cluster II was a space mission of the European Space Agency, with NASA participation, to study the Earth's magnetosphere over the course of nearly two solar cycles. The mission was composed of four identical spacecraft flying in a tetrahedral formation. As a replacement for the original Cluster spacecraft which were lost in a launch failure in 1996, the four Cluster II spacecraft were successfully launched in pairs in July and August 2000 onboard two Soyuz-Fregat rockets from Baikonur, Kazakhstan. In February 2011, Cluster II celebrated 10 years of successful scientific operations in space. In February 2021, Cluster II celebrated 20 years of successful scientific operations in space. As of March 2023, its mission was extended until September 2024. The China National Space Administration/ESA Double Star mission operated alongside Cluster II from 2004 to 2007.

The Nuclear End Run
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The threat of nuclear terrorism is about to take a leap forward because of a  deal now being worked out by Russia, France and Germany in which Russia will supply highly enriched bomb-grade uranium for use in research reactors. This is  the kind of fuel used in the Hiroshima bomb. 

     The deal foils the goal of the recent nuclear-safety summit meeting in Moscow, held to improve controls over Russian nuclear materials. It is also a slap at the United States because it circumvents our embargo on exports of bomb-grade uranium.

Netanyahu, Past and Future
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Benjamin Netanyahu has the potential to be most effective Israeli Prime Minister since David Ben-Gurion. I know this from having closely watched Mr. Netanyahu's brilliant work as Israel's permanent representative to the United Nations. 

     He also has the advantage of being the first Prime Minister directly elected  by the Israeli people. This means that once he has formed a coalition government, it cannot be brought down by a snap vote in the Knesset.

Florence Fabricant's Lobster Fra Diavolo
nytimes_articles.abstract: “Lobster Fra Diavolo, lobster in a spicy tomato sauce with linguine, ‘brother devil’ style, sounds Italian, tastes Italian and is a staple in Italian restaurants. But is it Italian?” Florence Fabricant set out to find out in a 1996 column. She spoke to a number of experts on Italian food, but came away with few answers. Some insisted it was an Italian-American dish, with roots in New York, specifically Long Island or Little Italy, while others pointed to Naples. She included this recipe, adapted from “Lobster at Home” by Jasper White, the executive chef of the Legal Sea Foods chain. His version is served on bread, but this adaptation is served on pasta. Florence Fabricant

nytimes_articles.headline: Blackberry Pie
nytimes_articles.abstract: This recipe came to The Times in 1996 from Annie Davis, a United Air Lines flight attendant from Elizabethton, Tenn., who competed in the Appalachian Fair in Tennessee. It is a classic summer pie, but don’t feel beholden to the season’s gifts: if the taste for blackberry pie strikes in, say, darkest January, feel free to bake it with frozen fruit. Use the opportunity to remind yourself that summer is, always and forever, on its way. Florence Fabricant

COMPANY BRIEFS
Cities of Despair
New Camcorder Features Allow the User to Play Director
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHILE the cost of gas goes through the roof, take solace: the camcorders used for saving vacation memories now have more power and more features, including even basic models. And many will bring the beach and mountains back home to the  living room for a discount price of around $500, instead of the $1,000 of a few  years ago. 

     As prices have dropped, camcorders have grown in popularity. According to the Electronics Industries Association, a trade group, nearly one in four families owns a camcorder and more than 3.5 million will be sold this year. Many of them  will record graduations, vacations, weddings and the baby's first steps, but they'll do it better, with built-in color screens and stereo sound and picture quality that surpasses a laser disk. Some will even allow you to edit tapes and  add special effects. (You pay more for the extras.)

Currents;Children's Book Artists as Muralists
nytimes_articles.abstract: FOUR children's book illustrators are creative partners in one of New York's  most colorful day-care centers: a 70-foot mural adorns the new headquarters of Women in Need, at 115 West 31st Street, designed by Jeffrey Murphy. The group provides housing for homeless women and their children. Rita Zimmer, its founder, said, "We have women who want to go back to work and who want to come in to the alcohol- and substance-abuse center for treatment but don't want to put their children in foster care. This center is for them." The mural, by Lisa  Desimini, Dan Yaccarino, Sara Schwartz and David Ricceri, is destined for a children's book.
